issues.title: test vectors
issues.author: redshiftzero
issues.body:
RFC 9180 in Appendix A has test vectors for the auth modes, as does https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-hpke-hpke-03 for the supported ciphersuites

Test vectors should be included, I think the test vectors from RFC 9180 should be bit identical to what we want since the same KEMs/KDFs/AEADs are used (from looking at Tables 2, 3, 5 in the two specs)

comments.author: cfm
comments.body: For inspiration: hpkewg/hpke#55...

issues.title: Consider documenting a generic construction
issues.author: bellebaum
issues.body:
I am relatively certain that such a thing would never be specified (because more-efficient-yet-less-generic alternatives will certainly arise before the need), but it is possible to generically construct an AuthKEM from an IND-CCA2 KEM and an EUF-CMA signature scheme.

In short: Public/Private keys are concatenated.

Encaps from sender S to the recipient R works as follows:

(c,k') <- KEM.Encaps(kemPkR)
s <- Signature.Sign(sigSkS, (c, idR))
k <- KDF(k', (c,s,idS))
output ciphertext (c,s) and shared key k

Decaps:

k' <- KEM.Decaps(kemSkR, c)
if k' is failure: output failure
v <- Signature.Verify(sigPkS, (c, idR), s)
if not v: output failure
k <- KDF(k', (c,s,idS))
output shared key k


Some notes:

- This scheme is sometimes explicitly rejecting and sometimes quiet, depending on the KEM, signature scheme and messages, but see below for modifications.
  - In the quiet case, HPKE will reject messages at the AEAD layer.
- idX is the "identity" of X, which can be the concatenated public keys in case those are unique. They ensure that the ciphertext is bound to a recipient (for integrity) and that the session key is bound to a sender (for confidentiality).
- The transcript hashing (inclusion of (c,s) in KDF) is partially optional (except for some modifications below), but I highly recommend it. It provides a certain safeguard against weak forgeries of c and s. If convenient, (c,s) may be pre-hashed using any collision resistant hash function. c and s never have to be stored in memory at the same time: c can be hashed and then sent/discarded before s is created/read.
- Hybrid KEMs and signatures can be freely used.

Special modifications:

- To get an explicitly rejecting AuthKEM:
  - The signature scheme has to be SUF-CMA.
  - The KEM has to be explicitly rejecting.
    - Alternative: Include KDF(k', "") in the input to the signature for key confirmation, which makes any quiet KEM explicitly rejecting.

- To get a guaranteed quiet AuthKEM:
  - The KEM has to be quiet.
    - Alternative: Use a secret fallback key k' as part of kemSk, although beware possible timing side channels!
  - The signature verification and KEM decapsulation SHOULD run in constant time irrespective of failure.
  - Remove all lines outputting failure and make v part of the KDF input. For Encaps, define v <- true

comments.author: rocodes
comments.body:
Thank you for taking the time to write up this proposal, and apologies for the tardy reply.

In our case, implicit authentication rather than signature-based authentication was the desirable property we wanted to retain; our motivation for proposing this draft is due to our use of HPKE in [securedrop-protocol](https://github.com/freedomofpress/securedrop-protocol), a whistleblowing protocol between anonymous sources and journalists, where we hope to retain a level of deniability similar conceptually to x3dh or noise IK. So a stronger form of sender authentication in AuthKem would work against us. If it is independently desirable we can document it, but it would not be something we would rely on ourselves. For now in the document we have noted the AuthKem construction found in https://eprint.iacr.org/2023/1480.

issues.title: emphasize transitional properties: PQ/T confidentiality, implicit traditional authentication
issues.author: cfm
issues.body:
Minor suggestions (put implicit sender auth mention first since it applies to both modes; slight shortening of rest of text), plus suggesting a sentence alluding to a justification for the mixed modes (transitional/deployment readiness) instead of "sufficient for many". (Not attached to the wording, just an idea):

> On top of these restored modes, this document defines DH-AKEM: named usage profiles of `mode_auth` and `mode_auth_psk` that expose the full HPKE context API (`Seal`, `Open`, and `Export`) to provide implicit sender authentication in the classical setting. When `mode_auth_psk` is used with the shared secret of a post-quantum KEM (PQ-KEM) encapsulation as the PSK, the construction provides hybrid PQ/T confidentiality, offering readily-deployable transitional security with off-the-shelf primitives.

_Originally posted by @rocodes in https://github.com/cfm/draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes/pull/1#discussion_r2916205665_

comments.author: rocodes
comments.body: Addressed in #19

issues.title: specific usage notes
issues.author: cfm
issues.body:
And then one more question: Should this be more generalized, i.e. should it be framed like:

- Auth, AuthPSK are the modes defined
- AuthPSK-transitional is a mode defined with a generic PQ secret (this maps to PSK mode in the hpke rfc, and that could be nice.) Assuming it's a properly generated PQ secret, the security assumptions are: the assumptions of the kdf, the pq kem, and the dh component)
  - Special mention: AuthPSK-transitional's PQ secret can be based on encapsulating a receiver PQ-KEM
  ? 

Finally: somewhere we need to mention that the KDF has to be a dual keyed kdf for the PSK modes? ~(which I assume is maybe mentioned somewhere in the Hpke PSK mode too)?~ edit: it's not: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9180.html#section-4-2.2.1

_Originally posted by @rocodes in https://github.com/cfm/draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes/pull/1#discussion_r2916279506_

comments.author: cfm
comments.body: @thomwiggers points out that this is not a PSK as typically understood: it's only receiver-authenticating, not mutually authenticating.

comments.author: rocodes
comments.body:
My opinion, esp based on the last comment, is to describe the general version (Auth, AuthPSK), treat AuthPSK-with-PQ-shared-secret as a subset of the latter, and not even mention the AuthPSK-with-receiver-PQ-KEM in the draft at all since it is kind of a "clever" misuse of the API that we are adapting to our own purposes and can justify ourselves, but might not merit being standardized. 

In the "Example applications" I would generalize the example to the PQ PSK and describe the transitional properties provided, but assume a real PSK, skip the receiver KEM parts.

What do you think?

issues.title: call profiles "transitional" rather than "hybrid"
issues.author: cfm
issues.body:
(Again, not to nit, but: would "DH-AKEM-Transitional" avoid overloading the term "hybrid" and emphasize the use case?)

_Originally posted by @rocodes in https://github.com/cfm/draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes/pull/1#discussion_r2916211482_

comments.author: rocodes
comments.body: If this works for you @cfm, I'll prepare a small PR, just with reference to the AuthPSK with PQ secret sub-case. (Can also do in conjunction with another change, such as generalizing the example application https://github.com/cfm/draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes/issues/15#issuecomment-4159429174)

comments.author: rocodes
comments.body: I think we can consider this closed via #19, where one profile was dropped and the other was reworked

issues.title: cite related work
issues.author: cfm
issues.body: [Shadowfax](https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/154.pdf) is of particular interest, but it's a new primitive, whereas this construction composes existing building blocks.

comments.author: rocodes
comments.body:
Also https://petsymposium.org/2018/files/papers/issue1/paper12-2018-1-source.pdf (re deniable authenticated key exchanges, re "transitional" security aka classical auth but PQ confidentiality);  potentially https://eprint.iacr.org/2024/890 (Gandalf / deniable ring signatures, some of same authors as shadowfax) 

Shadowfax emphasizes 2 KEMs + hybrid aspect plus deniability, but if we focus on the classical construction and mention that the PQ / transitional construction is a sub-case, we should clarify in what context we're referring to shadowfax, especially if we don't mention the second KEM (https://github.com/cfm/draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes/issues/15#issuecomment-4159429174)

issues.title: Mention deniability / more strongly mention implicit authentication
issues.author: rocodes
issues.body:
The word "deniable"/"deniability" should probably appear in the draft (possibly where KCI is mentioned?), because part of the purpose of preserving the auth and authpsk modes despite the KCI tradeoff is to allow for implicit authentication/deniability, and that informs use cases for this draft

Related: #17

comments.author: rocodes
comments.body: Addressed in #19

pulls.title: docs: initial copy-paste reframing of RFC 9180's `mode_auth` and `mode_auth_psk` via `draft-ietf-hpke-hpke-03`
pulls.author: cfm
pulls.body: Facilitated by Claude.  This will need some further reframing (and copyediting), but it should achieve the `draft-dh-akem-extension = RFC 9180 - draft-ietf-hpke-hpke` substance we're looking for.

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
reviews.body:
## Pull request overview This PR turns `draft-dh-akem-extension.md` from a template stub into an initial Internet-Draft that specifies a DH-AKEM construction by restoring HPKE authenticated modes (`mode_auth`, `mode_auth_psk`) and defining named usage profiles, including a hybrid PQ/classical variant. **Changes:** - Replaces placeholder front-matter and adds normative/informative references. - Adds an abstract/introduction plus a full technical specification (mode identifiers, `AuthEncap`/`AuthDecap`, setup functions, hybrid construction, and ciphersuite table). - Adds Security Considerations, IANA Considerations, and Acknowledgments content. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

You can also share your feedback on Copilot code review. [Take the survey](https://www.surveymonkey.com/r/XP6L3XJ).

comments.body: The abstract (and later sections) refer to “revision -03 of {{I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}}”, but the `I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke` citation isn’t version-pinned and HPKE is published as RFC 9180. Consider updating references/text so readers can unambiguously locate the exact spec being discussed (e.g., cite RFC 9180 for the base spec and, if needed, add an explicit reference to draft-ietf-hpke-hpke-03 when discussing what changed in -03).

This section says “No existing algorithm in {{I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}} is modified”, but the document later replaces the `VerifyPSKInputs` algorithm with an updated version. If the intent is “no behavior change for existing modes”, consider rephrasing to avoid contradicting the subsequent `VerifyPSKInputs` update.
```suggestion
-03 required to restore `mode_auth` and `mode_auth_psk`. These extensions
are defined so that the externally observable behavior of existing HPKE
modes is unchanged, although this document updates the `VerifyPSKInputs`
procedure in {{I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}}.
```

The cross-reference sentence appears reversed: `KeySchedule` is defined in {{!I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}}, while `AuthEncap`/`AuthDecap` are defined in {{sec-authencap}}. Swapping these references would make the provenance of each function unambiguous.
```suggestion
as defined in {{!I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}} and {{sec-authencap}} respectively.
```
Done for now in eebaeabb749d117e790b79668b604b70d6a9a34e, pending #5.

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
reviews.body:
## Pull request overview Copilot reviewed 1 out of 1 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

You can also share your feedback on Copilot code review. [Take the survey](https://www.surveymonkey.com/r/XP6L3XJ).

comments.body:
RFC 9180 is not an "informational" RFC (it’s Standards Track). Also, an Internet-Draft does not itself "supersede" an RFC until published; consider rephrasing to "successor to" / "intended to obsolete/update" RFC 9180 to avoid an inaccurate status statement.
```suggestion
{{I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}} is a standards-track Internet-Draft that is
intended as the standards-track successor to {{?RFC9180}} and omits
`mode_auth` (0x02) and `mode_auth_psk` (0x03). This document restores
those two modes as a strict extension, requiring only the addition of
`AuthEncap`/`AuthDecap` to the DHKEM, four Setup functions, and an update to `VerifyPSKInputs`.
```

Same issue as the abstract: RFC 9180 is Standards Track (not informational), and describing the Internet-Draft as having already superseded RFC 9180 is misleading. Rephrase to reflect the current publication status (e.g., successor / intended to obsolete).

The pseudocode function name `DH-AKEM-Hybrid.SetupS` includes hyphens, which can be read as subtraction and is inconsistent with the identifier style used elsewhere in the algorithms (e.g., `SetupAuthS`). Consider renaming to an unambiguous identifier (no `-`) and keep naming consistent across the document.
```suggestion
def DH_AKEM_Hybrid.SetupS(pkR, pkR_pq, skS, info):
  pq_ss, enc_pq = PQKEM.Encap(pkR_pq)
  enc_dh, ctx = SetupAuthPSKS(pkR, info, pq_ss, enc_pq, skS)
  return concat(enc_dh, enc_pq), ctx

def DH_AKEM_Hybrid.SetupR(enc, skR, skR_pq, pkS, info):
```

`DH-AKEM-Hybrid.SetupR` has the same hyphenated identifier issue as `SetupS`, and it also places `info` last whereas the other Setup functions in this document consistently take `info` immediately after the receiver/sender key inputs. Align the parameter ordering with the other Setup APIs to make misuse less likely.
```suggestion
def DH_AKEM_Hybrid_SetupS(pkR, info, pkR_pq, skS):
  pq_ss, enc_pq = PQKEM.Encap(pkR_pq)
  enc_dh, ctx = SetupAuthPSKS(pkR, info, pq_ss, enc_pq, skS)
  return concat(enc_dh, enc_pq), ctx

def DH_AKEM_Hybrid_SetupR(enc, skR, pkS, info, skR_pq):
```

reviews.body: sorry, this review got away from me. Here are the comments I made before (for posterity?).

comments.body:
Minor suggestions (put implicit sender auth mention first since it applies to both modes; slight shortening of rest of text), plus suggesting a sentence alluding to a justification for the mixed modes (transitional/deployment readiness) instead of "sufficient for many". (Not attached to the wording, just an idea):

> On top of these restored modes, this document defines DH-AKEM: named usage profiles of `mode_auth` and `mode_auth_psk` that expose the full HPKE context API (`Seal`, `Open`, and `Export`) to provide implicit sender authentication in the classical setting. When `mode_auth_psk` is used with the shared secret of a post-quantum KEM (PQ-KEM) encapsulation as the PSK, the construction provides hybrid PQ/T confidentiality, offering readily-deployable transitional security with off-the-shelf primitives.
(Again, not to nit, but: would "DH-AKEM-Transitional" avoid overloading the term "hybrid" and emphasize the use case?)

And then one more question: Should this be more generalized, i.e. should it be framed like:

- Auth, AuthPSK are the modes defined
- AuthPSK-transitional is a mode defined with a generic PQ secret (this maps to PSK mode in the hpke rfc, and that could be nice.) Assuming it's a properly generated PQ secret, the security assumptions are: the assumptions of the kdf, the pq kem, and the dh component)
  - Special mention: AuthPSK-transitional's PQ secret can be based on encapsulating a receiver PQ-KEM
  ? 

Finally: somewhere we need to mention that the KDF has to be a dual keyed kdf for the PSK modes? ~(which I assume is maybe mentioned somewhere in the Hpke PSK mode too)?~ edit: it's not: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9180.html#section-4-2.2.1
(I haven't checked the KEM and KDF IDs so I'm taking your word for it. But do we need to define these specific algorithms, or clarify choice of combination (p256 vs x25519)
Thanks, @rocodes.  I think #8 has already helped push in this direction, but I'll revise further along these lines in #14 and #15.
Filed in #16.

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
reviews.body:
## Pull request overview This PR renames/replaces the existing Internet-Draft with a new draft focused on restoring HPKE authenticated modes (`mode_auth` and `mode_auth_psk`) as a strict extension to `draft-ietf-hpke-hpke`, and re-frames the prior DH-AKEM material as informative examples/profiles. **Changes:** - Adds `draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes.md`, specifying `AuthEncap`/`AuthDecap`, updated `VerifyPSKInputs`, and four authenticated Setup functions. - Adds informative guidance for a hybrid PQ/T construction using `mode_auth_psk` with a PQ-KEM shared secret as the PSK, plus suggested parameter profiles. - Removes the prior `draft-dh-akem-extension.md` document. ### Reviewed changes Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes.md | New primary draft defining authenticated HPKE mode extensions and informative example applications/profiles. | | draft-dh-akem-extension.md | Deleted prior draft document that this PR replaces/supersedes. | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

You can also share your feedback on Copilot code review. [Take the survey](https://www.surveymonkey.com/r/XP6L3XJ).

comments.body: The `venue.github` value still points at `cfm/draft-dh-akem-extension`, which no longer matches this document’s `docname`/filename (`draft-ms-hpke-auth-modes`). This will produce incorrect Editor’s Copy/GitHub Pages links in rendered output; update it to the correct repository/path for this draft.

In the hybrid profile, the text says to include `pkR_pq` in `info`, but it doesn’t specify a deterministic byte encoding for `pkR_pq`. Since `info` is a byte string, please state how `pkR_pq` is serialized (and ideally length-delimited) to avoid ambiguity and interoperability issues.

`concat(info, pkR_pq)` in the HybridSetup pseudocode concatenates a public-key object into a byte string without defining its encoding. Align the pseudocode with the spec text by explicitly serializing `pkR_pq` (and consider length-prefixing) so both sides compute identical `info` bytes.

reviews.author: redshiftzero
reviews.body: looks good! few info level comments, feel free to disregard or punt until later

comments.body:
should be section 9.5? section 9.5 contains the entropy requirements:

> In the PSK modes, the PSK MUST have at least 32 bytes of entropy and SHOULD be of length Nh bytes or longer.

(though 9.7.5 discusses the impact of bad ephemeral randomness)
```suggestion
entropy requirement in {{Section 9.5 of !I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}} (32 bytes
```

so in RFC 9180, they have this section on validation of inputs and outputs: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9180#name-validation-of-inputs-and-ou

as does the new HPKE draft: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-hpke-hpke-03#name-validation-of-inputs-and-ou

comparing those, the authenticated modes have an additional requirement: 
> in authenticated modes, the recipient MUST validate the sender's static public key `pkS`. Validation failure yields a `ValidationError`

```suggestion
## Input Validation and Error Handling

In addition to the validation requirements in {{Section 7.1.4 of
!I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}}, the recipient MUST validate the sender's
static public key `pkS` before use in `AuthDecap()`, applying the
same validation rules as for other public key inputs. Validation failure
MUST yield a `ValidationError`.
```

perhaps worth adding a note about KCI here too (as RFC 9180 does):


```suggestion
Note that the AuthEncap() and AuthDecap() functions are vulnerable to key-compromise impersonation (KCI): the assurance that the shared secret was generated by the holder of the private key `skS` does not hold if the recipient private key `skR` is compromised. See {{sec-security}} for further discussion.
```

technically it's un-reserving those values, so maybe: 

```suggestion
The reserved entries for values 0x02 and 0x03 in Table 1 of {{!I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}} are replaced with the following mode identifiers: 
```

````suggestion
The reserved entries for values 0x02 and 0x03 in Table 1 of {{!I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}} are replaced with the following mode identifiers:
````

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
reviews.body:
## Pull request overview This PR updates the Internet-Draft text for “Authenticated Modes for HPKE”, primarily improving editorial clarity and updating references around restoring `mode_auth` and `mode_auth_psk` as an extension to the HPKE specification. **Changes:** - Refines the abstract and introduction text for clarity and updated framing (including hybrid PQ/T motivation). - Adds an informative reference for Alwen et al. (ASIACRYPT 2023) and updates Security Considerations language to reference RFC9180 sections. - Adjusts document metadata (keywords, author full name) and applies various formatting/consistency edits across pseudocode and prose. --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.

You can also share your feedback on Copilot code review. [Take the survey](https://www.surveymonkey.com/r/XP6L3XJ).

comments.body: Citations to the base HPKE spec use `{{I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}}` here (no `!`/`?` marker), but elsewhere the document uses `{{!I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}}`. Please make the citation marker consistent throughout (and ensure the reference is correctly classified as normative vs. informative).

`KeyScheduleS()`/`KeyScheduleR` mixes a function name with and without `()`. For consistency (and to avoid implying different identifiers), use the same convention for both (either include `()` on both or neither).

`Export_*()` is not defined elsewhere in this draft, and HPKE APIs are typically named `Export()` (singular). Consider using the exact function name(s) from {{I-D.ietf-hpke-hpke}} / {{?RFC9180}} to avoid introducing an ambiguous or non-existent API identifier.

pulls.title: Revisions: AuthPSK focus, split out `pskAPKE` from extension and treat as informative.
pulls.author: rocodes
pulls.body:
- Drop `mode_auth` with explanation that it cannot be constructed with quantum safety; treat it like building block to auth_psk
- Treat `mode_auth_psk` with a "true" PSK/pq shared secret, as the focus of the extension
  - Informatively offer `pskAPKE`, but drop the algorithms/parameters since it's just an example; include authentication caveats
  - Drop `mode_auth` references, treat identifier as reserved
- Add Motivation section describing the use-case, and add references to related work mentioning PSK for quantum-safe transition
- Fixup a few small issues (info param composition, adjust param ordering)
- Add @lsd-cat as an author

reviews.author: cfm
reviews.body: I like this framing a lot.  Thanks, @rocodes and @lsd-cat!

comments.body: Although this `psk` value is **not** a true pre-shared key, ....

"Prior to setting up an HPKE encryption context (either via `Setup` or via a single-shot API), the sender uses receiver's PQ public key `pkR_pq` to generate a shared secret and its encapsulation (`ss_pq`, `enc_pq`), and uses `ss_pq` as the `psk` and a static identifier as the PSK identifier. "

nit- Double-quotes around "psk" was intentional even if you want to format it monospaced; it's not a true psk and I think that it explains the second half of the sentence, although the prior mention is more important
Here I'd keep the double-quotes around "psk" even if it's monospaced, because it indicates that the term "psk" is a bit of an abuse but we are aware, as we then justify.
Thanks!  Squashed in 1ab8471382a53e6b4e6d5625123f125a0e6895dd.
Oops; thanks for catching this!  Squashed in 1ab8471382a53e6b4e6d5625123f125a0e6895dd.

Totally.  I'm torn about this because I think the air quotes are both a little informal and a little inaccurate: it *is* the `psk` value; it's just a "PSK" rather than a PSK.  But I've split the difference in 1ab8471382a53e6b4e6d5625123f125a0e6895dd; let's see what feedback we get.
